name: US Presidents in Order
description: All 47 US presidents in chronological order. Learn mode shows the full list; Quiz mode tests recall by number.

questions.question: Who was the 1st President of the United States?
questions.explanation: George Washington (no party/independent, 1789-1797) served with Vice President John Adams. A Virginia planter and surveyor, he commanded the Continental Army to victory in the Revolutionary War, crossing the Delaware on Christmas night 1776 for a surprise attack at Trenton. He presided over the Constitutional Convention in 1787 and was unanimously elected president by the Electoral College -- twice, the only president to achieve that distinction. He established crucial precedents including the two-term tradition, the Cabinet system, and the title "Mr. President" (rejecting grander alternatives). He signed the Judiciary Act of 1789 creating the federal court system and issued the Proclamation of Neutrality keeping America out of European wars. Washington voluntarily stepped down after two terms, an act King George III reportedly called the mark of the greatest man in the world. He famously warned against political parties and foreign entanglements in his Farewell Address. At death he was the wealthiest president in history relative to the economy. His false teeth were not wooden -- they were made from hippopotamus ivory, bone, and metal. He is the only president who never lived in the White House, as it was not completed until 1800. He was also the only sitting president to personally lead troops in the field, suppressing the Whiskey Rebellion in 1794.

questions.question: Who was the 2nd President of the United States?
questions.explanation: John Adams (Federalist, 1797-1801) served with Vice President Thomas Jefferson, who was his political rival -- under the original Electoral College system, the runner-up became VP. A Harvard-educated lawyer from Massachusetts, Adams was a key architect of independence, nominating Washington as commander-in-chief and helping draft the Declaration of Independence alongside Jefferson and Franklin. He served as the first American ambassador to Great Britain and was Washington's VP for eight years, calling it "the most insignificant office that ever the invention of man contrived." As president he avoided full-scale war with France during the Quasi-War, a decision he considered his greatest accomplishment but which cost him politically. He signed the controversial Alien and Sedition Acts, which criminalized criticism of the government. Adams was the first president to live in the White House, moving in November 1800 while it was still unfinished. He lost re-election to Jefferson in the bitter election of 1800 and left Washington before the inauguration, skipping the ceremony. He and Jefferson reconciled in old age through famous correspondence and both died on July 4, 1826 -- the 50th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence. Adams's purported last words were "Thomas Jefferson survives," not knowing Jefferson had died hours earlier. His son John Quincy Adams became the 6th president, making them the first father-son presidential pair.

questions.question: Who was the 3rd President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Thomas Jefferson (Democratic-Republican, 1801-1809) served with Vice Presidents Aaron Burr (first term) and George Clinton (second term). A Virginia planter, architect, and polymath, Jefferson was the principal author of the Declaration of Independence at age 33. He served as governor of Virginia, minister to France, Washington's first Secretary of State, and Adams's vice president before winning the presidency in the contentious election of 1800, resolved by the House of Representatives after an Electoral College tie with Burr. His greatest presidential achievement was the Louisiana Purchase in 1803, doubling the nation's size for about $15 million (roughly 3 cents per acre) and commissioning the Lewis and Clark Expedition to explore it. He signed the Act Prohibiting Importation of Slaves in 1807 and fought the First Barbary War against North African pirates, sending the Marines to "the shores of Tripoli." Jefferson founded the University of Virginia after leaving office and designed its campus. He could read five languages and his personal library became the foundation of the Library of Congress after the British burned the original in 1814. He died on July 4, 1826, the same day as John Adams and the 50th anniversary of the Declaration. His epitaph, which he wrote himself, omits the presidency entirely, listing instead the Declaration, Virginia's statute for religious freedom, and founding UVA. He is the only president whose vice president (Burr) killed a man (Alexander Hamilton) in a duel while in office.

questions.question: Who was the 4th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: James Madison (Democratic-Republican, 1809-1817) served with Vice Presidents George Clinton (who died in office in 1812) and Elbridge Gerry (who also died in office in 1814, giving us the word "gerrymander" from his creative redistricting as Massachusetts governor). Known as the "Father of the Constitution," Madison was the principal architect of both the Constitution and the Bill of Rights at the 1787 Constitutional Convention. A Princeton graduate, he co-authored The Federalist Papers with Hamilton and Jay, served in the Virginia legislature and U.S. House, and was Jefferson's Secretary of State. His presidency was defined by the War of 1812 against Britain, during which the British captured and burned Washington, D.C. in August 1814 -- making him the only sitting president to face enemy forces in the capital. His wife Dolley famously rescued Gilbert Stuart's portrait of George Washington from the White House before the British set it ablaze. The war ended with the Treaty of Ghent and Andrew Jackson's victory at New Orleans, boosting American nationalism. At 5 feet 4 inches and barely 100 pounds, Madison was the smallest president in U.S. history. He is the last Founding Father to die, passing away in 1836. Madison and his wife were known for lavish White House entertaining, with Dolley essentially inventing the role of First Lady as a public figure.

questions.question: Who was the 5th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: James Monroe (Democratic-Republican, 1817-1825) served with Vice President Daniel D. Tompkins. Monroe had one of the most distinguished pre-presidential resumes in American history: he fought in the Revolution (wounded at the Battle of Trenton), served as U.S. Senator, Governor of Virginia, Minister to France and Britain, and simultaneously held the positions of Secretary of State and Secretary of War under Madison. His presidency was called the "Era of Good Feelings" because the Federalist Party had collapsed, leaving the Democratic-Republicans essentially unopposed. He won re-election in 1820 with all but one electoral vote -- legend says a New Hampshire elector voted against him to preserve Washington's record of unanimity, though the elector later said he simply thought Monroe was a poor president. The Monroe Doctrine of 1823 declared the Western Hemisphere closed to European colonization, a cornerstone of U.S. foreign policy for two centuries. He presided over the Missouri Compromise of 1820, admitting Missouri as a slave state and Maine as free. Monroe was the last president who was a Founding Father, the last of the "Virginia Dynasty" (four of the first five presidents were Virginians), and the last president to wear knee breeches and powdered wigs. He died on July 4, 1831, making him the third president to die on Independence Day. The capital of Liberia, Monrovia, is named after him.

questions.question: Who was the 6th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: John Quincy Adams (Democratic-Republican/National Republican, 1825-1829) served with Vice President John C. Calhoun. The son of President John Adams, he was arguably the most qualified person ever to assume the presidency: he served as minister to the Netherlands, Prussia, Russia, and Britain, as a U.S. Senator from Massachusetts, and as Secretary of State under Monroe, where he was the primary author of the Monroe Doctrine. He won the presidency in the controversial 1824 election despite losing both the popular vote and electoral vote to Andrew Jackson -- the House of Representatives chose him after Henry Clay threw his support behind Adams, leading Jackson's supporters to cry "corrupt bargain" when Adams then appointed Clay Secretary of State. His one-term presidency was largely stymied by Jackson's allies in Congress. After losing re-election to Jackson in 1828, Adams did something unprecedented: he won a seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in 1830 and served 17 years, earning the nickname "Old Man Eloquent." He became a fierce opponent of slavery, leading the fight to overturn the "gag rule" that prohibited discussion of anti-slavery petitions in Congress. He successfully argued the Amistad case before the Supreme Court, winning freedom for enslaved Africans. He collapsed on the House floor from a stroke on February 21, 1848, and died two days later in the Speaker's Room of the Capitol -- the only former president to die in the Capitol building. He kept a detailed diary for nearly 70 years, one of the most valuable primary sources in American history.

questions.question: Who was the 7th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Andrew Jackson (Democrat, 1829-1837) served with Vice Presidents John C. Calhoun (who resigned in 1832, the first VP to do so) and Martin Van Buren. Jackson was a self-made frontier lawyer, judge, and military hero who became famous for his decisive victory at the Battle of New Orleans in 1815. He served in both the House and Senate from Tennessee before his presidency. As president, Jackson founded what became the modern Democratic Party and championed "Jacksonian democracy," expanding voting rights for white men while pursuing brutal policies toward Native Americans, most notoriously the Indian Removal Act of 1830 that led to the Trail of Tears. He destroyed the Second Bank of the United States by vetoing its recharter and withdrawing federal funds, a fight that defined his presidency. He survived the first assassination attempt on a sitting president in 1835 when both of Richard Lawrence's pistols misfired -- Jackson reportedly beat the man with his cane. During the Nullification Crisis, he faced down South Carolina's attempt to void federal tariffs, threatening military force and preserving the Union. Jackson fought at least 13 duels in his lifetime, killing Charles Dickinson in 1806 and carrying bullets in his body for decades. He was the first president born in a log cabin and the first from west of the Appalachians. His nickname "Old Hickory" came from troops who said he was as tough as old hickory wood. He adopted two Native American boys, a fact often overlooked given his devastating Indian removal policies.

questions.question: Who was the 8th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Martin Van Buren (Democrat, 1837-1841) served with Vice President Richard Mentor Johnson, who was controversial for his open relationship with a biracial woman. Van Buren was a masterful New York politician who served as state attorney general, U.S. Senator, Governor of New York (briefly), Secretary of State, Minister to Britain, and Vice President under Jackson before winning the presidency in 1836. He was the first president born as a U.S. citizen rather than a British subject, and he grew up speaking Dutch in Kinderhook, New York -- English was his second language. His presidency was dominated by the Panic of 1837, a severe economic depression that earned him the derisive nickname "Martin Van Ruin." He established the Independent Treasury system to manage government funds without a national bank. He lost re-election to William Henry Harrison in 1840 in a campaign famous for the "Tippecanoe and Tyler Too" slogan. Van Buren ran again in 1848 as the Free Soil Party candidate, becoming an anti-slavery elder statesman despite having earlier been a Democratic Party machine politician. He is credited with co-founding the Democratic Party alongside Andrew Jackson and is widely considered the first professional politician to become president. The expression "OK" is popularly (though disputedly) said to derive from his nickname "Old Kinderhook." His autobiography, remarkably, never mentions his wife Hannah, who died 18 years before he became president.

questions.question: Who was the 9th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: William Henry Harrison (Whig, 1841) served with Vice President John Tyler for just 31 days -- the shortest presidency in American history. Born into Virginia aristocracy as the son of Benjamin Harrison V (a signer of the Declaration of Independence), he studied medicine briefly before joining the Army and fighting in the Northwest Indian War. He served as the first territorial governor of Indiana for 12 years, a U.S. Representative and Senator from Ohio, and minister to Colombia. His fame rested on his 1811 victory at the Battle of Tippecanoe against Tecumseh's confederacy, giving him the nickname "Old Tippecanoe" and the immortal campaign slogan "Tippecanoe and Tyler Too." At 68, he was the oldest person elected president until Ronald Reagan in 1980. He delivered the longest inaugural address in history -- 8,445 words lasting nearly two hours -- on a cold, rainy March day without wearing an overcoat or hat. He developed pneumonia (though modern historians debate whether the speech caused it) and died on April 4, 1841. His death triggered a constitutional crisis because the document was ambiguous about whether the vice president became president or merely acting president -- Tyler's assertion that he was fully president set the precedent followed ever since. Harrison was the last president born as a British subject and the grandfather of Benjamin Harrison, the 23rd president, making them the only grandfather-grandson presidential pair. His time in office was so brief that he made no significant policy decisions whatsoever.

questions.question: Who was the 10th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: John Tyler (Whig/no party, 1841-1845) had no vice president for his entire term, as there was no mechanism to fill the vacancy. A Virginia lawyer and states' rights advocate, Tyler served as a U.S. Representative, Governor of Virginia, and U.S. Senator before being placed on the Whig ticket to attract Southern votes. He was the first vice president to assume the presidency upon a president's death, and his insistence that he was the actual president (not merely "acting president") set a crucial constitutional precedent, though critics called him "His Accidency." He quickly vetoed the Whig agenda including re-establishing a national bank, causing his entire cabinet (except Secretary of State Daniel Webster) to resign in a single day -- the only mass cabinet resignation in U.S. history. The Whig Party formally expelled him, and he was nearly impeached by the House. Despite this chaos, he annexed Texas by joint resolution in his final days in office, a major territorial expansion. Tyler had 15 children by two wives -- more than any other president. Remarkably, because his children were born over such a long span, two of his grandsons were still alive in the 2020s, a staggering fact given Tyler was born in 1790. After leaving office, Tyler sided with the Confederacy and was elected to the Confederate House of Representatives, making him the only former president to commit what the U.S. government considered treason. He died in 1862 before taking his seat, and his death was the only presidential death not officially recognized in Washington.

questions.question: Who was the 11th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: James K. Polk (Democrat, 1845-1849) served with Vice President George M. Dallas (for whom Dallas, Texas is named). A protege of Andrew Jackson from Tennessee, Polk served in the U.S. House for 14 years including as Speaker (the only Speaker to later become president) and one term as Governor of Tennessee. He entered the 1844 Democratic convention as a dark horse candidate and became the first surprise nominee in American political history. Polk set four ambitious goals and achieved all of them: he reduced tariffs, re-established the Independent Treasury, acquired California from Mexico, and settled the Oregon boundary dispute with Britain at the 49th parallel. The Mexican-American War (1846-1848) was the defining event of his presidency, resulting in the massive Mexican Cession that added California, Nevada, Utah, and parts of Arizona, New Mexico, Wyoming, and Colorado to the United States. Critics including Abraham Lincoln and Ulysses Grant called it an unjust war of aggression. Polk was a relentless micromanager who worked himself to exhaustion and pledged to serve only one term. He kept that promise, declined to run again, and died just 103 days after leaving office at age 53 -- the shortest post-presidency in history. He is the only president who also served as Speaker of the House. Historians consistently rank him among the most effective one-term presidents despite his relative obscurity with the general public.

questions.question: Who was the 12th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Zachary Taylor (Whig, 1849-1850) served with Vice President Millard Fillmore. A career Army officer for nearly 40 years, Taylor earned the nickname "Old Rough and Ready" for his informal dress and willingness to share hardships with his troops. He became a national hero through victories in the Mexican-American War, particularly at the battles of Palo Alto, Resaca de la Palma, and Buena Vista, where he defeated a force three times larger than his own. Taylor had never held political office, had never voted in an election, and had no clearly stated political positions when the Whigs nominated him purely on his military fame. Despite being a Louisiana slaveholder who owned over 100 enslaved people, he surprised both sides by opposing the expansion of slavery into new territories, threatening to personally lead the Army to enforce federal authority if Southern states attempted secession -- a decade before Lincoln faced the same crisis. He died on July 9, 1850, just 16 months into his presidency, after falling ill at a July 4th ceremony at the Washington Monument (then under construction). The traditional story is that he gorged on cherries and iced milk on a hot day, developing acute gastroenteritis. His body was exhumed in 1991 to test for arsenic poisoning; the results were negative. Taylor was the second president to die in office, the last Whig elected president, and the last president to own enslaved people while in office. His son-in-law was Jefferson Davis, the future president of the Confederacy.

questions.question: Who was the 13th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Millard Fillmore (Whig, 1850-1853) had no vice president, as there was still no succession mechanism for filling the vacancy. Born into poverty in a log cabin in upstate New York, Fillmore was largely self-educated, apprenticed to a cloth maker as a teenager, and rose through sheer determination to become a lawyer and congressman. He served four terms in the U.S. House, including as chairman of the Ways and Means Committee, and was elected New York State Comptroller before being chosen as Taylor's running mate. His most consequential act as president was signing the Compromise of 1850, crafted by Henry Clay and Stephen Douglas, which admitted California as a free state but included the Fugitive Slave Act requiring Northerners to return escaped enslaved people -- a law that enraged abolitionists and arguably hastened the Civil War. He opened trade with Japan by sending Commodore Matthew Perry's expedition in 1852, though Perry arrived after Fillmore left office. He was the last Whig president and the last president who was neither a Democrat nor a Republican. After failing to win the Whig nomination in 1852, he ran for president in 1856 as the candidate of the nativist Know-Nothing (American) Party, carrying only Maryland. Fillmore installed the first bathtub and the first kitchen stove in the White House. He reportedly refused an honorary degree from Oxford University because it was in Latin and he said he should not accept a degree he could not read.

questions.question: Who was the 14th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Franklin Pierce (Democrat, 1853-1857) served with Vice President William Rufus DeVane King, who was so ill he took the oath of office in Cuba (the only time this has happened abroad) and died just 45 days into the term, leaving the vice presidency vacant for nearly four years. Pierce was a New Hampshire lawyer who served in both the U.S. House and Senate before resigning to practice law, then served as a brigadier general in the Mexican-American War. He was a dark horse nominee, chosen on the 49th ballot at the Democratic convention. Two months before his inauguration, his 11-year-old son Benjamin was killed in a train wreck before his parents' eyes, a trauma from which neither Pierce nor his wife Jane ever recovered -- she wore black throughout the presidency and he turned to heavy drinking. Pierce signed the Kansas-Nebraska Act of 1854, which repealed the Missouri Compromise and opened territories to popular sovereignty on slavery, directly causing "Bleeding Kansas" and accelerating the path to Civil War. He enforced the Fugitive Slave Act so vigorously that he alienated his own party and became the only elected president to be denied renomination by his party for a second term. Pierce was the first president to have a Christmas tree in the White House and the first to deliver his inaugural address from memory. His college roommate at Bowdoin was Nathaniel Hawthorne, who remained his lifelong friend and wrote his campaign biography. After leaving office, Pierce became so unpopular for his pro-Southern sympathies that when Lincoln was assassinated, a mob gathered outside his home demanding to know why he had not hung a black crepe.

questions.question: Who was the 15th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: James Buchanan (Democrat, 1857-1861) served with Vice President John C. Breckinridge, who at 36 was the youngest VP in history and would later become a Confederate general. A Pennsylvania lawyer with decades of experience, Buchanan served in the U.S. House, as minister to Russia, as a U.S. Senator for 10 years, as Secretary of State under Polk, and as minister to Britain. He is the only president who never married; his orphaned niece Harriet Lane served as White House hostess. His close relationship with Alabama Senator William Rufus King (Pierce's VP) led to contemporary speculation about his sexuality -- Andrew Jackson called them "Miss Nancy and Aunt Fancy." Buchanan's presidency is widely considered the worst in American history for his failure to address the secession crisis. He believed secession was illegal but also believed the federal government had no constitutional authority to stop it, effectively doing nothing as seven Southern states left the Union after Lincoln's election. He supported the Dred Scott decision and tried to admit Kansas as a slave state under the fraudulent Lecompton Constitution, splitting his own party. By the time he left office in March 1861, the Confederate States had already formed a government. His famous quote to Lincoln at the inauguration -- "If you are as happy entering the presidency as I am leaving it, then you are a very happy man" -- captures his relief at escaping office. He spent his retirement defending his record and died in 1868, still insisting history would vindicate him.

questions.question: Who was the 16th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Abraham Lincoln (Republican, 1861-1865) served with Vice Presidents Hannibal Hamlin (first term) and Andrew Johnson (second term). Born in a Kentucky log cabin, Lincoln was largely self-educated, became a lawyer and state legislator in Illinois, served one term in the U.S. House, and gained national fame through the 1858 Lincoln-Douglas debates despite losing that Senate race. He won the four-way 1860 presidential election with just 39.8% of the popular vote -- the lowest winning percentage ever -- without appearing on the ballot in most Southern states. He preserved the Union through the Civil War (1861-1865), the bloodiest conflict in American history with over 600,000 dead. The Emancipation Proclamation of 1863 freed enslaved people in Confederate states and transformed the war into a fight for human freedom. He pushed through the 13th Amendment abolishing slavery entirely, signed the Homestead Act and the Morrill Land-Grant Act, and established the transcontinental railroad. His Gettysburg Address -- just 272 words -- is considered one of the greatest speeches in the English language. At 6 feet 4 inches, he remains the tallest president. He was assassinated by actor John Wilkes Booth at Ford's Theatre on April 14, 1865 (Good Friday), just five days after Lee's surrender, and died the next morning. Lincoln is one of only three presidents consistently ranked #1 by historians (with Washington and FDR). He held a patent for a device to lift boats over shoals, making him the only president to hold a patent. He was also an accomplished wrestler in his youth, reportedly losing only one match in roughly 300 bouts.

questions.question: Who was the 17th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Andrew Johnson (National Union/Democrat, 1865-1869) had no vice president for his entire term. Born into extreme poverty in Raleigh, North Carolina, Johnson never attended school and was apprenticed to a tailor at age 10. His wife Eliza taught him to read and write as a teenager, making him the most poorly educated president. He rose through Tennessee politics as a champion of poor whites, serving as alderman, mayor, state legislator, U.S. Representative (five terms), Governor of Tennessee, and U.S. Senator. He was the only Southern senator to remain loyal to the Union during the Civil War, which made him Lincoln's running mate on the unity ticket in 1864. Johnson assumed the presidency after Lincoln's assassination and immediately clashed with the Radical Republicans over Reconstruction, vetoing the Civil Rights Act of 1866 (Congress overrode him) and opposing the 14th Amendment. He was the first president impeached by the House, charged with violating the Tenure of Office Act by firing Secretary of War Edwin Stanton. He survived removal by a single Senate vote -- Senator Edmund Ross of Kansas cast the deciding not-guilty vote, destroying his own career. Johnson's lenient Reconstruction policies allowed former Confederates to regain power and pass Black Codes restricting freedoms of formerly enslaved people, shaping the Jim Crow era. He appeared drunk at his vice presidential inauguration, which scandalized Washington. After leaving office, he returned to the U.S. Senate in 1875 -- the only former president to serve in the Senate after the presidency -- but died five months later. He requested to be buried wrapped in an American flag with his head resting on a copy of the Constitution.

questions.question: Who was the 18th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Ulysses S. Grant (Republican, 1869-1877) served with Vice Presidents Schuyler Colfax (first term) and Henry Wilson (second term, who died in office in 1875). Born Hiram Ulysses Grant in Ohio, the "S" in his famous name came from a clerical error by his congressman when nominating him to West Point -- Grant kept it because he liked the initials "U.S." He graduated from West Point and served with distinction in the Mexican-American War but left the Army in 1854 amid rumors of heavy drinking and failed at several civilian careers before the Civil War. He rose from obscurity to become the Union's commanding general, capturing Fort Donelson, winning at Shiloh, taking Vicksburg in a brilliant campaign, and ultimately accepting Lee's surrender at Appomattox. As president, Grant aggressively enforced Reconstruction and crushed the first Ku Klux Klan through the Enforcement Acts and the creation of the Department of Justice. He signed the 15th Amendment guaranteeing voting rights regardless of race, established Yellowstone as the first national park, and pursued a peaceful "Peace Policy" toward Native Americans. His presidency was marred by scandals among his appointees, including Credit Mobilier and the Whiskey Ring, though Grant himself was never personally corrupt. After leaving office, a con man swindled him out of his fortune, and he wrote his celebrated Personal Memoirs while dying of throat cancer, finishing them just days before his death -- Mark Twain published the book, which earned his family the equivalent of millions. Grant's funeral procession in New York City was seven miles long, one of the largest public gatherings in American history at that time.

questions.question: Who was the 19th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Rutherford B. Hayes (Republican, 1877-1881) served with Vice President William A. Wheeler, whom Hayes reportedly had never met before the convention. A Harvard Law School graduate from Ohio, Hayes was a staunch abolitionist who defended fugitive slaves in court, served with distinction as a Union officer in the Civil War (wounded five times, reaching brevet major general), and served in the U.S. House and three terms as Governor of Ohio. He won the most disputed election in American history: he lost the popular vote to Democrat Samuel Tilden by about 250,000 votes and initially appeared to lose the Electoral College, but disputed returns from three Southern states were resolved by a 15-member bipartisan commission that awarded all contested votes to Hayes on an 8-7 party-line vote. The Compromise of 1877 gave Hayes the presidency in exchange for withdrawing federal troops from the South, effectively ending Reconstruction and earning him the derisive nicknames "Rutherfraud" and "His Fraudulency." As president, he fought the spoils system by battling Roscoe Conkling's New York patronage machine, reformed the civil service, and vetoed the Bland-Allison Act (overridden). His wife Lucy was nicknamed "Lemonade Lucy" because she banned alcohol from the White House -- she was also the first First Lady with a college degree. Hayes pledged to serve only one term and kept that promise. He championed public education after leaving office and was an early advocate for federal aid to education. He was the first president to have a telephone installed in the White House and the first to visit the West Coast while in office.

questions.question: Who was the 20th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: James A. Garfield (Republican, 1881) served with Vice President Chester A. Arthur. Born in a log cabin in Ohio -- the last president with that distinction -- Garfield grew up fatherless and impoverished but became a classics professor, college president (of what is now Hiram College at age 26), lay preacher in the Disciples of Christ church, and Civil War general before entering politics. He served nine terms in the U.S. House and was the only sitting House member ever elected president; he had also been elected to the Senate by Ohio's legislature but never took that seat. He was a dark horse nominee, chosen on the 36th ballot at the Republican convention. Garfield could simultaneously write in Latin with one hand and Greek with the other, a talent he reportedly demonstrated at parties. He was shot on July 2, 1881 -- just four months into his presidency -- by Charles Guiteau, a delusional office-seeker, at the Baltimore and Potomac Railroad station in Washington. Garfield lingered for 80 agonizing days as doctors repeatedly probed the wound with unsterilized instruments, likely causing the fatal infection. Alexander Graham Bell even tried to locate the bullet with an electromagnetic device but failed because of the metal bed springs. He died on September 19, 1881, at age 49. His assassination directly led to civil service reform, as public outrage over the spoils system that motivated Guiteau fueled passage of the Pendleton Act under his successor Arthur. Garfield is the only president to go directly from the House of Representatives to the White House.

questions.question: Who was the 21st President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Chester A. Arthur (Republican, 1881-1885) had no vice president during his term. A Vermont-born lawyer who became a New York political operative, Arthur was deeply embedded in the Roscoe Conkling patronage machine, serving as Collector of the Port of New York -- the most lucrative patronage position in the federal government -- until President Hayes fired him for corruption. He was placed on the ticket with Garfield as a sop to the Stalwart faction, and virtually no one expected him to become president. His transformation in office is one of the great surprises of presidential history: the former spoilsman signed the Pendleton Civil Service Reform Act of 1883, creating merit-based federal hiring and directly attacking the system that had made his career. He also signed the first federal immigration law (the Immigration Act of 1882), the Chinese Exclusion Act (one of the most restrictive immigration laws in U.S. history), and oversaw the modernization and expansion of the U.S. Navy. Known as "Elegant Arthur" and the "Gentleman Boss," he owned 80 pairs of pants and reportedly changed clothes multiple times daily. He had a Tiffany-designed redecoration of the White House and sold 24 wagonloads of historic furniture at auction, horrifying preservationists. Arthur suffered from Bright's disease (a kidney condition) during his presidency and kept it secret from the public, which partly explains why he did not vigorously pursue the 1884 nomination. He died just 20 months after leaving office. Mark Twain said of him: "It would be hard indeed to better President Arthur's administration."

questions.question: Who was the 22nd President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Grover Cleveland (Democrat, 1885-1889, first term) served with Vice President Thomas A. Hendricks, who died eight months into the term. Born Stephen Grover Cleveland in New Jersey, he dropped his first name early in life. He rose meteorically through New York politics: from sheriff of Erie County to Mayor of Buffalo to Governor of New York to president in just three years, earning a reputation as an honest reformer who fought Tammany Hall corruption. He was the first Democrat elected president after the Civil War, breaking 24 years of Republican control. During the 1884 campaign, he was hit with the scandal that he had fathered an illegitimate child; rather than deny it, he admitted responsibility ("Tell the truth"), and voters respected his honesty. He married 21-year-old Frances Folsom in the White House in 1886, becoming the only president to marry in the executive mansion and, at the time, the only president to marry while in office (Tyler had as well). Frances was the youngest First Lady in history at 21. Cleveland was a fierce advocate of the gold standard and low tariffs, vetoed more bills than all previous presidents combined (mostly fraudulent pension claims), and signed the Interstate Commerce Act creating the first federal regulatory agency. He lost re-election in 1888 to Benjamin Harrison despite winning the popular vote -- his wife reportedly told the White House staff to take care of the furniture because they would be back in four years. She was right.

questions.question: Who was the 23rd President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Benjamin Harrison (Republican, 1889-1893) served with Vice President Levi P. Morton. The grandson of President William Henry Harrison (the 9th president) and great-grandson of Benjamin Harrison V (a signer of the Declaration of Independence), he is the only president who was a grandson of another president. An Indianapolis lawyer and Civil War veteran who rose to brevet brigadier general, Harrison served as a U.S. Senator from Indiana. He won the 1888 election despite losing the popular vote to Grover Cleveland by about 90,000 votes, one of only five presidents to win without the popular vote. His presidency saw significant legislative activity: the Sherman Antitrust Act (the first federal antitrust law), the McKinley Tariff (the highest protective tariff to that point), the Sherman Silver Purchase Act, and the admission of six new states (North Dakota, South Dakota, Montana, Washington, Idaho, and Wyoming) -- more than any other president. He also signed legislation creating the first national forest reserves. The White House was first wired for electricity during his presidency, but Harrison and his family were reportedly afraid to touch the switches and sometimes slept with the lights on. He was known as "the human iceberg" for his cold personality -- one supporter said Harrison could make a room full of supporters feel like they had been given an ice bath. His wife Caroline died of tuberculosis in the White House two weeks before the 1892 election, which he lost in a rematch to Cleveland. He later married his wife's niece, Mary Dimmick, causing a family scandal.

questions.question: Who was the 24th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Grover Cleveland (Democrat, 1893-1897, second term) served with Vice President Adlai Stevenson I (grandfather of the 1952 and 1956 Democratic nominee). Cleveland is the only president to serve two non-consecutive terms, making him both the 22nd and 24th president -- the reason the U.S. has had 46 individuals but 47 presidencies. His second term was far more turbulent than his first: the Panic of 1893 struck almost immediately, triggering the worst economic depression the country had experienced to that point. He repealed the Sherman Silver Purchase Act to protect the gold standard and controversially had to arrange a private gold loan from J.P. Morgan to prevent the Treasury from going bankrupt. He sent federal troops to break the Pullman Strike of 1894 over the objections of Illinois Governor John Peter Altgeld, a move that alienated organized labor. Cleveland opposed the annexation of Hawaii after discovering the 1893 overthrow of Queen Liliuokalani had been aided by American officials. In the summer of 1893, he secretly underwent surgery for oral cancer on his friend's yacht, the Oneida, in Long Island Sound -- the tumor and part of his upper jaw were removed, and the public was told he merely had a toothache. The secret was not fully revealed until 1917, nine years after his death. By 1896, his own party had turned against him so completely that the Democrats nominated William Jennings Bryan, who represented everything Cleveland opposed. Cleveland retired to Princeton, New Jersey, became a trustee of Princeton University, and died in 1908. His last words were reportedly: "I have tried so hard to do right."

questions.question: Who was the 25th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: William McKinley (Republican, 1897-1901) served with Vice Presidents Garret Hobart (who died in office in 1899) and Theodore Roosevelt (second term). An Ohio lawyer and Civil War veteran who enlisted as a private and rose to brevet major (earning the nickname "Major McKinley" for life), he served 14 years in the U.S. House as a champion of protective tariffs and one term as Governor of Ohio. His 1896 campaign against William Jennings Bryan was managed by Mark Hanna, the first modern political campaign strategist, and McKinley ran the first "front porch" campaign, delivering speeches to delegations who traveled to his Canton, Ohio home. He led the nation into the Spanish-American War of 1898, a brief conflict that resulted in Spain ceding Puerto Rico, Guam, and the Philippines and the U.S. annexing Hawaii, transforming America into an imperial power almost overnight. He also pursued the Open Door Policy in China to ensure equal trading rights. McKinley won re-election in an 1900 rematch with Bryan but was shot on September 6, 1901 by anarchist Leon Czolgosz at the Pan-American Exposition in Buffalo, New York. He died eight days later, the third president to be assassinated. McKinley led a political realignment that made the Republicans the dominant party for decades, and his presidency marked America's emergence as a world power. He was the last president to have served in the Civil War and the last to have been born before the Civil War. His face appeared on the $500 bill. His assassination spurred the Secret Service to formally take over presidential protection.

questions.question: Who was the 26th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Theodore Roosevelt (Republican, 1901-1909) served with Vice President Charles W. Fairbanks (second term; he had no VP during his first partial term). Born into a wealthy New York family, TR overcame severe childhood asthma through sheer will, becoming a boxer, wrestler, rancher, and outdoorsman. He graduated from Harvard, served in the New York State Assembly, was a U.S. Civil Service Commissioner, New York City Police Commissioner, Assistant Secretary of the Navy, colonel of the Rough Riders (leading the famous charge up San Juan Hill in the Spanish-American War), Governor of New York, and Vice President before assuming the presidency at 42 after McKinley's assassination -- the youngest person to become president. He won election in his own right in 1904 by the largest popular vote margin to that point. Roosevelt was the driving force of the Progressive Era: he busted over 40 monopolistic trusts, signed the Pure Food and Drug Act and Meat Inspection Act (inspired by Upton Sinclair's The Jungle), created the U.S. Forest Service, established 150 national forests and 5 national parks, and built the Panama Canal. He won the 1906 Nobel Peace Prize for mediating the end of the Russo-Japanese War, the first American to win any Nobel Prize. His foreign policy motto was "speak softly and carry a big stick." In 1912, running as the Progressive ("Bull Moose") Party candidate, he was shot in the chest before a speech in Milwaukee; the bullet was slowed by his folded 50-page speech and metal glasses case, and he delivered the 90-minute address bleeding from the wound, declaring "it takes more than that to kill a Bull Moose." He was also the first president to ride in an automobile, fly in an airplane, and go underwater in a submarine. The teddy bear is named after him.

questions.question: Who was the 27th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: William Howard Taft (Republican, 1909-1913) served with Vice President James S. Sherman (who died in office just before the 1912 election). A Cincinnati lawyer and jurist, Taft served as a federal circuit judge, the first civilian Governor-General of the Philippines, and Secretary of War under Roosevelt, who handpicked him as his successor. Taft actually busted more trusts in four years than Roosevelt had in seven, and he supported the 16th Amendment (income tax) and 17th Amendment (direct election of senators), both ratified after he left office. He signed legislation creating the Department of Labor and expanded the federal civil service. But he fractured the Republican Party by firing Roosevelt's ally Gifford Pinchot as Chief Forester and signing the Payne-Aldrich Tariff, which progressives saw as a betrayal. Roosevelt challenged him for the 1912 nomination, and when he failed, ran as a third-party candidate, splitting the Republican vote and handing the election to Woodrow Wilson -- Taft finished third, carrying only Utah and Vermont, the worst showing by an incumbent president. Taft is the only person in American history to serve as both president and Chief Justice of the Supreme Court; he was appointed by Warren Harding in 1921 and served until 1930, calling it his greatest honor. At over 300 pounds, he was the heaviest president and allegedly got stuck in the White House bathtub (a story that may be apocryphal, though he did order a specially sized tub). He was the first president to throw a ceremonial first pitch at a baseball game and the first to have a presidential automobile. Taft is buried at Arlington National Cemetery, one of only two presidents interred there (JFK is the other).

questions.question: Who was the 28th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Woodrow Wilson (Democrat, 1913-1921) served with Vice President Thomas R. Marshall, remembered for the quip "What this country needs is a good five-cent cigar." Wilson was a political science professor and president of Princeton University who became Governor of New Jersey in 1910 with no prior political experience, then won the presidency just two years later -- the only president with an earned doctorate (PhD from Johns Hopkins in political science). He signed sweeping Progressive legislation: the Federal Reserve Act creating the modern banking system, the Federal Trade Commission Act, the Clayton Antitrust Act, and the first permanent federal income tax. He won re-election in 1916 on the slogan "He kept us out of war," then led America into World War I in April 1917, declaring the world must be "made safe for democracy." After the Allied victory, he personally negotiated at the Paris Peace Conference and championed the League of Nations, for which he won the 1919 Nobel Peace Prize. But the Senate, led by Henry Cabot Lodge, refused to ratify the Treaty of Versailles. While campaigning for the League, Wilson suffered a massive stroke in October 1919 that left him partially paralyzed; his wife Edith effectively ran the executive branch for the remaining 17 months of his presidency in what some call the first female presidency, a secret so well-kept that the vice president was never informed of the severity. Wilson was an ardent segregationist who re-segregated the federal civil service and screened Birth of a Nation at the White House. He is the only president buried in Washington, D.C. (at the National Cathedral). His first wife Ellen died in the White House in 1914, and he married Edith Bolling Galt in 1915.

questions.question: Who was the 29th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Warren G. Harding (Republican, 1921-1923) served with Vice President Calvin Coolidge. A newspaper publisher from Marion, Ohio, Harding served as an Ohio state senator, Lieutenant Governor of Ohio, and U.S. Senator before winning the presidency in a 1920 landslide with over 60% of the popular vote -- the first election in which women could vote nationally. He campaigned on a "return to normalcy" after the upheaval of WWI and Wilson's idealism. He signed the Budget and Accounting Act creating the first formal federal budget process, established the Bureau of Veterans Affairs, hosted the Washington Naval Conference that achieved the first international arms limitation agreement, and signed immigration restriction legislation. He appointed former president Taft as Chief Justice and appointed conservative stalwarts to his cabinet. However, his presidency became synonymous with scandal after his death: the Teapot Dome affair, in which Interior Secretary Albert Fall leased naval oil reserves to private companies in exchange for bribes, was the biggest government scandal before Watergate. Attorney General Harry Daugherty was also implicated in corruption. Harding died suddenly on August 2, 1923, in San Francisco, officially of a heart attack or stroke, though conspiracy theories about poisoning by his wife Florence persisted for decades (she refused to allow an autopsy). He carried on extramarital affairs including with Nan Britton, who claimed he fathered her daughter (DNA testing in 2015 confirmed this), and with Carrie Fulton Phillips. Harding is consistently ranked among the worst presidents, though recent historians have begun a modest rehabilitation of his record. He was the first sitting senator elected president and the first president to visit Alaska.

questions.question: Who was the 30th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Calvin Coolidge (Republican, 1923-1929) served with Vice President Charles G. Dawes (who won the Nobel Peace Prize for the Dawes Plan restructuring German war reparations). A Vermont-born lawyer who built his political career in Massachusetts, Coolidge served as a city councilman, state legislator, mayor of Northampton, state senator (and senate president), Lieutenant Governor, and Governor of Massachusetts, where he gained national fame by breaking the 1919 Boston Police Strike, declaring "There is no right to strike against the public safety by anybody, anywhere, any time." He was sworn in as president by his father, a notary public, by kerosene lamplight in the family farmhouse in Plymouth Notch, Vermont upon learning of Harding's death -- one of the most iconic scenes in presidential history. Coolidge's governing philosophy was radical non-intervention: he slashed taxes, reduced the national debt by a third, cut government spending, and presided over the Roaring Twenties boom. He signed the Immigration Act of 1924 establishing strict national origin quotas and the Indian Citizenship Act granting citizenship to all Native Americans. Nicknamed "Silent Cal" for his legendary taciturnity, he was the subject of countless anecdotes: when a dinner guest said she had bet she could get him to say more than two words, he replied "You lose." Dorothy Parker, told he had died, reportedly asked "How can they tell?" Despite the jokes, he held more press conferences than any president before or since. His 16-year-old son Calvin Jr. died of blood poisoning from a blister acquired playing tennis on the White House court in 1924, a loss Coolidge said took the "power and the glory" out of the presidency. He declined to run in 1928 with the cryptic announcement "I do not choose to run" and retired to Northampton. He died of a heart attack in 1933 at age 60.

questions.question: Who was the 31st President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Herbert Hoover (Republican, 1929-1933) served with Vice President Charles Curtis, the first person with significant Native American (Kaw Nation) ancestry to hold national office and the last VP born in a log cabin. Hoover was orphaned at age nine in Iowa, worked his way through Stanford's first graduating class as a geology major, and became a fabulously wealthy mining engineer working on five continents. He organized massive humanitarian relief during and after World War I, feeding millions in Belgium through the Commission for Relief in Belgium and directing the U.S. Food Administration, earning the title "The Great Humanitarian" and making him one of the most admired people in the world. He served as Secretary of Commerce under Harding and Coolidge, transforming it into a powerful agency. He won the 1928 election in a landslide over Al Smith, the first Catholic major-party nominee. Then the stock market crashed on October 29, 1929, just eight months into his term, beginning the Great Depression. Hoover was not the do-nothing president of popular myth -- he created the Reconstruction Finance Corporation, signed the Smoot-Hawley Tariff (which most economists believe worsened the depression), and started construction of the Hoover Dam -- but his philosophy of voluntary cooperation and limited government intervention proved tragically inadequate. Shantytowns of the homeless were called "Hoovervilles," newspapers used as blankets were "Hoover blankets," and empty pockets turned inside out were "Hoover flags." He lost to FDR in a 1932 landslide, carrying only six states. Hoover lived another 31 years -- the longest post-presidency until Jimmy Carter -- and rehabilitated his reputation through public service, heading two bipartisan commissions on government reorganization under Truman and Eisenhower. He was fluent in Mandarin Chinese, which he and his wife Lou spoke in the White House when they wanted privacy.

questions.question: Who was the 32nd President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Franklin D. Roosevelt (Democrat, 1933-1945) served with three vice presidents: John Nance Garner (1933-1941), Henry A. Wallace (1941-1945), and Harry S. Truman (1945). Born into a wealthy New York family and a distant cousin of Theodore Roosevelt (whose niece Eleanor he married), FDR served as a New York state senator, Assistant Secretary of the Navy, and Governor of New York. He was paralyzed from the waist down by polio (or possibly Guillain-Barre syndrome) in 1921 at age 39 but concealed the extent of his disability from the public -- the press cooperated, and only two photographs exist of him in a wheelchair. He is the only president elected four times, serving an unprecedented 12 years, which led to the 22nd Amendment limiting presidents to two terms. His New Deal programs -- the CCC, WPA, TVA, Social Security, the SEC, the FDIC, and dozens more -- fundamentally transformed the relationship between the federal government and its citizens. He led America through most of World War II, delivering the famous "date which will live in infamy" speech after Pearl Harbor, forging the Allied alliance with Churchill and Stalin, and directing the war effort. He authorized the Manhattan Project that developed the atomic bomb. FDR died of a cerebral hemorrhage at Warm Springs, Georgia on April 12, 1945, just weeks before Germany's surrender, while sitting for a portrait with his mistress Lucy Mercer Rutherfurd. His "fireside chats" pioneered the use of radio for direct presidential communication. He appointed the first woman to the cabinet (Frances Perkins as Secretary of Labor) and nominated eight Supreme Court justices, more than any president except Washington. He is consistently ranked among the top three presidents in American history alongside Lincoln and Washington.

questions.question: Who was the 33rd President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Harry S. Truman (Democrat, 1945-1953) served with Vice President Alben W. Barkley (second term; he had no VP during his first partial term). The "S" in his name is his full middle name -- it does not stand for anything, honoring both grandfathers (Anderson Shipp Truman and Solomon Young), which is why some style guides write it without a period. A Missouri farmer, haberdasher (his men's clothing store went bankrupt), and WWI artillery captain, Truman entered politics through the Pendergast political machine, serving as a county judge and U.S. Senator before becoming FDR's VP in 1945. He became president just 82 days later when FDR died and learned about the atomic bomb for the first time. He made the decision to drop atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki, ending WWII. His presidency shaped the postwar world: the Truman Doctrine committed the U.S. to containing communism, the Marshall Plan rebuilt Western Europe, the Berlin Airlift kept West Berlin free, NATO was founded, and the Korean War was fought to stalemate. He desegregated the Armed Forces by executive order in 1948 and recognized Israel within minutes of its declaration of independence. He won the 1948 election in the greatest upset in American presidential history, defeating Thomas Dewey despite polls and the famous "Dewey Defeats Truman" headline. He survived an assassination attempt by Puerto Rican nationalists in 1950. A sign on his desk read "The Buck Stops Here." He left office with a 32% approval rating -- one of the lowest ever -- but historians now rank him among the top ten presidents. He was the last president without a college degree.

questions.question: Who was the 34th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Dwight D. Eisenhower (Republican, 1953-1961) served with Vice President Richard Nixon. Born in Denison, Texas and raised in Abilene, Kansas, Eisenhower graduated from West Point and spent decades in the Army, rising to become Supreme Allied Commander in Europe during WWII, planning and directing the D-Day invasion of Normandy on June 6, 1944 -- the largest amphibious military operation in history. After the war, he served as Army Chief of Staff, president of Columbia University, and the first Supreme Allied Commander of NATO. Both parties courted him for the 1952 nomination; he chose the Republicans and won in a landslide. As president, he ended the Korean War within six months, managed Cold War tensions with the Soviet Union (including the Suez Crisis and the U-2 incident), signed the Federal Aid Highway Act of 1956 creating the Interstate Highway System (officially justified as a defense measure), sent federal troops to Little Rock, Arkansas to enforce school desegregation in 1957, signed the first civil rights legislation since Reconstruction (the Civil Rights Acts of 1957 and 1960), established NASA after Sputnik, and signed legislation making Hawaii and Alaska the 49th and 50th states. His farewell address warning of the "military-industrial complex" remains one of the most prophetic speeches in American political history. Eisenhower was an avid golfer who played over 800 rounds during his presidency, and a squirrel he trapped on the White House putting green became national news. He was the first president limited to two terms by the 22nd Amendment. He had a secret wartime relationship with his driver, Kay Summersby. His campaign slogan "I Like Ike" is one of the most famous in American political history.

questions.question: Who was the 35th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: John F. Kennedy (Democrat, 1961-1963) served with Vice President Lyndon B. Johnson. Born into one of America's wealthiest and most politically connected families, JFK graduated from Harvard, served as a Navy PT boat commander in the Pacific during WWII (his boat PT-109 was rammed and sunk by a Japanese destroyer, and he heroically saved his crew), and served in both the U.S. House and Senate from Massachusetts. At 43, he was the youngest person elected president (Theodore Roosevelt was younger when he succeeded McKinley but was not elected at that age) and the first Catholic to hold the office, overcoming intense anti-Catholic prejudice. His inauguration speech contained the famous line "Ask not what your country can do for you -- ask what you can do for your country." His presidency saw some of the Cold War's tensest moments: the failed Bay of Pigs invasion, the Berlin Wall's construction, and the Cuban Missile Crisis of October 1962, the closest the world has come to nuclear war. He established the Peace Corps, championed the space program (pledging to land a man on the moon by decade's end), and proposed landmark civil rights legislation. He negotiated the Limited Nuclear Test Ban Treaty with the Soviet Union. Kennedy was assassinated on November 22, 1963, in Dallas, Texas by Lee Harvey Oswald while riding in a motorcade through Dealey Plaza -- a moment seared into the national memory. The Warren Commission concluded Oswald acted alone, though conspiracy theories have proliferated ever since. His brother Robert was assassinated in 1968 while running for president. JFK won a Pulitzer Prize for Profiles in Courage (though ghostwriting allegations persist) and is buried at Arlington National Cemetery beneath an eternal flame.

questions.question: Who was the 36th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Lyndon B. Johnson (Democrat, 1963-1969) served with Vice President Hubert H. Humphrey (second term; no VP during his first partial term). A Texan who taught briefly at a Mexican-American school (an experience he said inspired his civil rights convictions), LBJ was elected to the U.S. House at 28, served in the Navy during WWII, and won a razor-thin 87-vote Senate primary in 1948 (earning the sarcastic nickname "Landslide Lyndon"). He became the youngest Senate Majority Leader in history and was the most powerful legislative tactician of his era. He was sworn in as president aboard Air Force One just hours after JFK's assassination, with Jackie Kennedy standing beside him still wearing her blood-stained pink suit. His "Great Society" produced some of the most transformative legislation in American history: the Civil Rights Act of 1964, the Voting Rights Act of 1965, Medicare, Medicaid, Head Start, the Immigration and Nationality Act of 1965 (abolishing national origin quotas), and federal aid to education. He won the 1964 election over Barry Goldwater with 61.1% of the popular vote, the highest since 1820. But the Vietnam War destroyed his presidency -- troop levels escalated from 16,000 advisors to over 500,000 combat troops, and the Tet Offensive in 1968 shattered public confidence. On March 31, 1968, he stunned the nation by announcing he would not seek re-election. LBJ was famously crude and domineering, often conducting meetings while using the toilet. He lifted his beagles by the ears for photographers, causing a national uproar. He died on January 22, 1973, just two days before the Vietnam ceasefire he had sought.

questions.question: Who was the 37th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Richard Nixon (Republican, 1969-1974) served with Vice Presidents Spiro Agnew (who resigned in 1973 over bribery charges) and Gerald Ford (appointed under the 25th Amendment). A California lawyer and Navy veteran, Nixon rose through Red Scare politics, gaining fame on the House Un-American Activities Committee for the Alger Hiss case, serving as senator and then as Eisenhower's VP for eight years. He lost the 1960 presidential election to JFK in one of the closest races ever, then lost the 1962 California governor's race, telling reporters "you won't have Nixon to kick around anymore." His 1968 comeback is one of the most remarkable in American politics. Nixon's foreign policy achievements were historic: he opened diplomatic relations with Communist China in 1972 (the "Nixon goes to China" moment that entered the American lexicon), negotiated detente and the SALT I arms treaty with the Soviet Union, and ended American involvement in the Vietnam War (though not before secretly bombing Cambodia). Domestically, he created the Environmental Protection Agency, signed the Clean Air Act, established OSHA, implemented the first affirmative action program (the Philadelphia Plan), and proposed a guaranteed minimum income. But Watergate -- the cover-up of a break-in at Democratic National Committee headquarters at the Watergate complex -- consumed his second term. Facing certain impeachment after the Supreme Court ordered release of the White House tapes, Nixon resigned on August 9, 1974, the only president to do so, delivering the famous departure speech: "Always remember, others may hate you, but those who hate you don't win unless you hate them, and then you destroy yourself." He was pardoned by Ford and spent two decades rehabilitating his image as an elder statesman. He appeared on the cover of Time magazine 55 times, more than any other person.

questions.question: Who was the 38th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Gerald Ford (Republican, 1974-1977) served with Vice President Nelson Rockefeller, making the Ford-Rockefeller administration the only one in which neither the president nor vice president had been elected to their position. Born Leslie Lynch King Jr. in Omaha, Nebraska, he was renamed after his stepfather Gerald Rudolff Ford who adopted him. Ford was a star football player at the University of Michigan (he turned down offers from the NFL's Detroit Lions and Green Bay Packers), attended Yale Law School, served as a Navy officer in the Pacific during WWII, and represented Michigan in the U.S. House for 25 years, including as House Minority Leader. He was appointed vice president under the 25th Amendment after Spiro Agnew's resignation in 1973, then became president when Nixon resigned in August 1974 -- the only person to serve as president without being elected either president or vice president. His pardon of Nixon on September 8, 1974 -- just one month into his presidency -- was hugely controversial and likely cost him the 1976 election, though he received the John F. Kennedy Profile in Courage Award in 2001 for the decision, which many came to view as necessary to help the nation heal. Ford survived two assassination attempts within 17 days in September 1975, both in California -- by Lynette "Squeaky" Fromme (a Manson family member) and Sara Jane Moore. Despite his athletic background, Ford was lampooned by Chevy Chase on Saturday Night Live as a clumsy bumbler, an image that stuck. He signed the Helsinki Accords and oversaw the final evacuation of Saigon. He lost the 1976 election to Jimmy Carter after barely surviving a primary challenge from Ronald Reagan. He lived to 93, the longest-lived president until Carter surpassed him.

questions.question: Who was the 39th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Jimmy Carter (Democrat, 1977-1981) served with Vice President Walter Mondale. A Naval Academy graduate who served on nuclear submarines under Admiral Hyman Rickover, Carter returned to Plains, Georgia to run the family peanut farm after his father's death. He served as a Georgia state senator and one term as Governor of Georgia before his improbable 1976 presidential campaign, running as a Washington outsider in the wake of Watergate with the promise "I will never lie to you." His greatest achievement was the Camp David Accords of 1978, personally mediating 13 days of negotiations between Egyptian President Anwar Sadat and Israeli Prime Minister Menachem Begin, producing the first peace treaty between Israel and an Arab state. He also negotiated the SALT II treaty with the Soviet Union, established the Departments of Energy and Education, and deregulated the airline, trucking, and brewing industries. But his presidency was plagued by the energy crisis, double-digit inflation, and the Iran hostage crisis -- 52 Americans held for 444 days after Iranian revolutionaries stormed the U.S. Embassy in Tehran. A failed rescue attempt (Operation Eagle Claw) was a humiliating disaster. He lost re-election to Ronald Reagan in a landslide. Carter's post-presidency is arguably the most distinguished of any president: he founded the Carter Center, monitored elections worldwide, helped eradicate Guinea worm disease, built houses with Habitat for Humanity, and won the 2002 Nobel Peace Prize. He was the first president born in a hospital, the first graduate of the Naval Academy to become president, and lived to be 100 years old -- the longest-lived president in American history. He taught Sunday school at Maranatha Baptist Church in Plains into his nineties.

questions.question: Who was the 40th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Ronald Reagan (Republican, 1981-1989) served with Vice President George H. W. Bush. A small-town Illinois boy who became a radio sportscaster, Hollywood actor (starring in over 50 films), president of the Screen Actors Guild, and two-term Governor of California, Reagan was the face of modern American conservatism. At 69, he was the oldest person elected president until Joe Biden, and he brought a former actor's communication skills to the role, earning the nickname "The Great Communicator." He survived an assassination attempt on March 30, 1981 -- just 69 days into his term -- when John Hinckley Jr. shot him outside the Washington Hilton. With a bullet lodged an inch from his heart, he quipped to surgeons: "I hope you're all Republicans." His economic program, dubbed "Reaganomics," combined massive tax cuts, deregulation, and increased military spending, producing both economic growth and large deficits. He fired 11,345 striking air traffic controllers (PATCO) in 1981, a defining moment for labor relations. His military buildup and tough rhetoric toward the Soviet Union -- calling it the "Evil Empire" and proposing the Strategic Defense Initiative ("Star Wars") -- combined with his later diplomacy with Mikhail Gorbachev and the INF Treaty, contributed to the end of the Cold War. The Iran-Contra scandal -- secretly selling arms to Iran and funneling profits to Nicaraguan rebels -- was the major blemish on his presidency. Reagan won re-election in 1984 in one of the most lopsided victories in history, carrying 49 of 50 states and 525 electoral votes. He disclosed his Alzheimer's diagnosis in 1994 in a handwritten letter to the nation and died in 2004. His "Mr. Gorbachev, tear down this wall" speech at the Brandenburg Gate in 1987 is one of the iconic moments of the Cold War.

questions.question: Who was the 41st President of the United States?
questions.explanation: George H. W. Bush (Republican, 1989-1993) served with Vice President Dan Quayle, famous for his gaffe of misspelling "potato" as "potatoe" at a school spelling bee. Bush had perhaps the most impressive pre-presidential resume of the 20th century: WWII Navy pilot (shot down over the Pacific at age 20, making him one of the youngest naval aviators), Yale graduate and Phi Beta Kappa member, Texas oilman, U.S. Representative, Ambassador to the United Nations, Chief of the U.S. Liaison Office in China, Director of the CIA, and eight-year Vice President under Reagan. He oversaw the end of the Cold War, the fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989, and the peaceful reunification of Germany. He assembled a 35-nation coalition for the Gulf War in 1991, liberating Kuwait from Iraq's Saddam Hussein in just 100 hours of ground combat -- a decisive military victory. He signed the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) and the Clean Air Act Amendments of 1990. He also signed the Immigration Act of 1990 increasing legal immigration. But his broken "Read my lips: no new taxes" pledge from the 1988 convention, combined with an economic recession and the third-party candidacy of Ross Perot (who won 19% of the vote), led to his defeat by Bill Clinton in 1992. Bush was a prolific skydiver, making tandem jumps on his 75th, 80th, 85th, and 90th birthdays. He and his son George W. Bush are the second father-son presidential pair after John and John Quincy Adams. He died on November 30, 2018, just months after his wife Barbara, making them the longest-married presidential couple (73 years). He was the last president to have served in WWII.

questions.question: Who was the 42nd President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Bill Clinton (Democrat, 1993-2001) served with Vice President Al Gore. Born William Jefferson Blythe III in Hope, Arkansas (his father died in a car accident three months before his birth, and he later took his stepfather's surname), Clinton was a Georgetown graduate, Rhodes Scholar at Oxford, and Yale Law School graduate. He served as Arkansas Attorney General and five terms as Governor of Arkansas (becoming the youngest governor in the country at 32). His centrist "Third Way" approach, known as Clintonism, moved the Democratic Party toward the center. He presided over the longest peacetime economic expansion in American history, with 22 million new jobs and the first budget surpluses in decades. He signed NAFTA, welfare reform (the Personal Responsibility and Work Opportunity Act), the Brady Bill (gun control), the Family and Medical Leave Act, and the Violent Crime Control Act. He negotiated the Oslo Accords and the Good Friday Agreement in Northern Ireland. Clinton was the second president impeached by the House (after Andrew Johnson), charged with perjury and obstruction of justice related to his affair with White House intern Monica Lewinsky and his testimony in the Paula Jones sexual harassment lawsuit. He was acquitted by the Senate in 1999 and left office with a 65% approval rating, one of the highest for any departing president. He was the first baby boomer president and famously played saxophone on The Arsenio Hall Show during the 1992 campaign. He was a notoriously late sleeper who could consume enormous amounts of fast food. His presidential library in Little Rock cost $165 million, and his post-presidency through the Clinton Foundation focused on global health and development. He is the third-youngest president, taking office at 46.

questions.question: Who was the 43rd President of the United States?
questions.explanation: George W. Bush (Republican, 2001-2009) served with Vice President Dick Cheney, widely considered the most powerful VP in American history. The eldest son of President George H. W. Bush, he graduated from Yale and Harvard Business School (the only president with an MBA), was a Texas Air National Guard pilot during the Vietnam era, ran an oil company, co-owned the Texas Rangers baseball team, and served as Governor of Texas. He won the presidency in the most controversial election since 1876: the 2000 race against Al Gore came down to Florida, where the outcome hinged on 537 votes and a Supreme Court decision (Bush v. Gore) that halted the recount on a 5-4 vote -- Gore won the popular vote by over 500,000. The September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks nine months into his presidency defined his tenure. He launched the War on Terror, invaded Afghanistan to topple the Taliban, created the Department of Homeland Security and the TSA, and signed the USA PATRIOT Act. The 2003 invasion of Iraq, justified by intelligence about weapons of mass destruction that proved false, became one of the most controversial foreign policy decisions in American history. He signed the No Child Left Behind Act (education reform with Ted Kennedy), the largest Medicare expansion since its creation (the prescription drug benefit), and PEPFAR, the AIDS relief program that saved millions of lives in Africa and is widely considered his greatest legacy. The 2008 financial crisis struck in his final year, prompting massive bank bailouts (TARP). He won re-election in 2004 against John Kerry. Bush is an avid painter in retirement, focusing on portraits of veterans and world leaders. He is the only president whose father also held the office in the modern era. He threw a perfect strike at Yankee Stadium in the 2001 World Series, a defining cultural moment after 9/11.

questions.question: Who was the 44th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Barack Obama (Democrat, 2009-2017) served with Vice President Joe Biden. Born in Honolulu, Hawaii to a Kenyan father and a Kansas mother, Obama was raised partly in Indonesia, graduated from Columbia University and Harvard Law School (where he was the first African American president of the Harvard Law Review), worked as a community organizer in Chicago, taught constitutional law at the University of Chicago, and served as an Illinois state senator and U.S. Senator from Illinois. He was the first African American president, winning in 2008 with the historic slogan "Yes We Can" and unprecedented grassroots fundraising. His signature legislative achievement was the Affordable Care Act ("Obamacare"), the most significant healthcare reform since Medicare, extending coverage to over 20 million uninsured Americans. He signed the Dodd-Frank Wall Street reform act, the Lilly Ledbetter Fair Pay Act, repealed "Don't Ask, Don't Tell" allowing openly gay military service, and the Supreme Court legalized same-sex marriage during his tenure. He ordered the Navy SEAL raid that killed Osama bin Laden on May 2, 2011, ended combat operations in Iraq, and negotiated the Iran nuclear deal (JCPOA) and the Paris Climate Agreement. He won the 2009 Nobel Peace Prize (controversially, as he had been in office less than a year). He won re-election in 2012 over Mitt Romney. Obama was the first president born outside the contiguous 48 states, the first to visit all 50 states while in office, and the first sitting president to visit Hiroshima. He is left-handed (the sixth of the last eight presidents to be left-handed, a statistical anomaly). His memoir Dreams from My Father was published in 1995, and A Promised Land became the bestselling presidential memoir. He reportedly carries a small collection of personal talismans in his pocket including a tiny Madonna and child.

questions.question: Who was the 45th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Donald Trump (Republican, 2017-2021, first term) served with Vice President Mike Pence. Born in Queens, New York, Trump graduated from the Wharton School at the University of Pennsylvania, took over his father's real estate business, and built a brand empire spanning hotels, casinos, golf courses, beauty pageants (Miss Universe), a bestselling book (The Art of the Deal), and the NBC reality show The Apprentice, which made him a household name. He was the first president with no prior political or military experience. He lost the popular vote to Hillary Clinton by nearly 3 million votes but won the Electoral College 304-227. His major legislative achievement was the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017, the largest tax overhaul in 30 years. He appointed three Supreme Court justices (Neil Gorsuch, Brett Kavanaugh, and Amy Coney Barrett), shifting the court decisively to the right. He renegotiated NAFTA into the USMCA, brokered the Abraham Accords normalizing relations between Israel and several Arab states, and initiated a trade war with China. His presidency was marked by extreme polarization and norm-breaking: he was impeached twice by the House -- first for pressuring Ukraine to investigate Biden (acquitted in February 2020) and second for incitement of insurrection following the January 6, 2021 storming of the Capitol (acquitted in February 2021). He is the only president impeached twice. The COVID-19 pandemic dominated his final year, and Operation Warp Speed produced vaccines in record time. He refused to concede the 2020 election and did not attend Biden's inauguration, the first outgoing president to skip the ceremony since Andrew Johnson in 1869. Trump became the first former president to be criminally indicted (in four separate cases beginning in 2023) and the first former president convicted of felony charges.

questions.question: Who was the 46th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Joe Biden (Democrat, 2021-2025) served with Vice President Kamala Harris, the first woman, first African American, and first Asian American to hold the office. Born in Scranton, Pennsylvania and raised in Delaware, Biden graduated from the University of Delaware and Syracuse Law School. He was first elected to the U.S. Senate from Delaware in 1972 at age 29 (just barely meeting the constitutional minimum of 30 by the time he was sworn in), and served 36 years as a senator, including as chairman of the Judiciary Committee (overseeing the Bork and Thomas Supreme Court hearings) and chairman of the Foreign Relations Committee. He was Barack Obama's vice president for eight years. Tragedy shaped his public life: his first wife Neilia and infant daughter Naomi were killed in a car accident in 1972, weeks after his first Senate election, and his son Beau died of brain cancer in 2015. At 78, he was the oldest person inaugurated as president. He signed the American Rescue Plan ($1.9 trillion COVID stimulus), the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (the largest infrastructure spending in decades), the CHIPS and Science Act, the Inflation Reduction Act (the largest climate investment in U.S. history), and the Bipartisan Safer Communities Act (the first major gun safety legislation in 30 years). He oversaw the U.S. withdrawal from Afghanistan (marked by chaos at Kabul airport), rallied NATO support for Ukraine after Russia's invasion, and managed historically low unemployment. He is known for his lifelong struggle with stuttering, which he has spoken about publicly to inspire others. He commuted daily by Amtrak between Wilmington and Washington during his Senate career, earning the nickname "Amtrak Joe." In July 2024, he made the historic decision to withdraw from the 2024 presidential race, the first sitting president to decline to seek re-election since LBJ in 1968.

questions.question: Who is the 47th President of the United States?
questions.explanation: Donald Trump (Republican, 2025-present, second term) serves with Vice President JD Vance, a venture capitalist and first-term U.S. Senator from Ohio who gained fame as the author of the bestselling memoir Hillbilly Elegy. Trump became only the second president in American history to serve non-consecutive terms, after Grover Cleveland (the 22nd and 24th president), and the first to accomplish this feat in over 130 years. He is the first president to win back the White House after losing re-election since Cleveland in 1892. He won the 2024 election against Vice President Kamala Harris, recapturing the popular vote as well as the Electoral College. His return to office was remarkable given the unprecedented circumstances: he ran while facing four criminal indictments, a felony conviction in New York, and being the first former president to have his home (Mar-a-Lago) searched by the FBI under a warrant. At 78 at the time of his second inauguration, he surpassed his own predecessor Joe Biden as the oldest person inaugurated. He survived an assassination attempt at a campaign rally in Butler, Pennsylvania on July 13, 2024, when a bullet grazed his ear, an image that became iconic during the campaign. Trump is the first president since the 19th century to return to the White House after a gap in service, and his second term has focused on immigration enforcement, tariff policy, government spending reduction through the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) led by Elon Musk, and reshaping the federal workforce. He is only the second person to be both a convicted felon and a sitting president.
